title: Land use bylaw overview
text: The City of Calgary land use bylaw establishes zoning districts that regulate how land can be used across the municipality. Residential districts range from R-1 single-detached to R-CG rowhouse configurations. Commercial districts include C-COR1 corridor commercial and C-N neighbourhood commercial. Mixed-use districts allow both residential and commercial activity on the same parcel, promoting walkable neighbourhoods. Each district specifies maximum building heights, setbacks from property lines, lot coverage ratios, and minimum parking requirements. Variances from these standards require a development permit application reviewed by the Calgary Planning Commission. The bylaw is updated periodically through public engagement processes where residents can provide input on proposed amendments. Recent amendments have focused on increasing density along transit corridors and enabling secondary suites in most residential areas. The bylaw interacts with the Municipal Development Plan to ensure that local zoning aligns with the city's long-term growth strategy.

title: Public transit strategic plan
text: Calgary Transit operates bus and light rail transit services across the city. The CTrain system has two lines: the Red Line running north-south and the Blue Line running northeast to west. The Green Line, currently under construction, will extend service to the southeast and north-central areas. Bus rapid transit routes on dedicated lanes serve high-demand corridors including 17th Avenue and the Airport Trail. The strategic plan targets a 30 percent increase in ridership by 2030 through frequency improvements, fare integration with regional partners, and last-mile connections. Peak service frequency on the CTrain is every 3 to 5 minutes during rush hours. The transit plan prioritizes accessibility by upgrading stations with elevators, tactile surfaces, and real-time passenger information displays. Fare policy uses a zone-based structure with monthly passes, day passes, and low-income transit passes available. The city also supports park-and-ride facilities at suburban CTrain stations to encourage multimodal commuting.

title: Water services and infrastructure
text: Calgary's water supply comes primarily from the Bow and Elbow rivers, treated at the Bearspaw and Glenmore water treatment plants. The city treats approximately 400 million litres of water per day during peak summer demand. Water quality meets or exceeds all Health Canada guidelines for Canadian drinking water. The wastewater system collects sewage through over 4,800 kilometres of sanitary sewer pipes and treats it at three wastewater treatment plants before discharging to the Bow River. The city's water efficiency plan aims to reduce per-capita consumption to 350 litres per day by 2033 through rebate programs for low-flow fixtures, outdoor watering restrictions during drought conditions, and public education campaigns. Stormwater management uses a combination of constructed wetlands, dry ponds, and underground storage to control runoff and protect downstream water quality. Infrastructure renewal budgets allocate approximately 200 million dollars annually to replace aging water mains and sewer lines, prioritizing areas with the highest break rates.

title: Affordable housing strategy
text: The city of Calgary affordable housing strategy sets a target of 4,500 new affordable units by 2030. Affordable housing is defined as costing no more than 30 percent of gross household income for renters earning below the median income. The strategy uses four main tools: direct capital grants to non-profit housing providers, inclusionary zoning that requires a percentage of affordable units in new developments over 20 units, density bonusing that allows extra floor area in exchange for affordable units, and land banking where the city acquires parcels in advance for future housing projects. The Non-Profit Housing Partnership Program provides operating subsidies to organizations managing existing affordable units. The city also works with the provincial and federal governments on cost-shared programs under the National Housing Strategy. Priority populations include Indigenous peoples, newcomers, seniors on fixed incomes, and people experiencing or at risk of homelessness. An annual report tracks progress against targets and identifies emerging housing needs through vacancy rate monitoring and waitlist data.

title: Parks and recreation master plan
text: Calgary maintains over 8,600 hectares of parkland including regional parks, community parks, and natural environment parks along the river valleys. The parks and recreation master plan guides investment in new facilities, trail connections, and programming. The pathway network spans over 1,000 kilometres of multi-use pathways connecting neighbourhoods to major destinations. Recreation facilities include 14 aquatic centres, 12 arenas, and over 200 sports fields managed by the city. The master plan emphasizes equitable access by locating new facilities in underserved communities identified through a service gap analysis. Natural area management protects native grasslands, wetlands, and riparian zones from development encroachment. The urban forest strategy targets an increase in tree canopy from 8 to 14 percent through street tree planting and developer requirements. Community associations partner with the city to maintain local parks, rinks, and tennis courts through volunteer agreements. Annual park maintenance budgets cover mowing, irrigation, playground inspections, and pathway repair.

title: Emergency management plan
text: The Calgary Emergency Management Agency coordinates the city's response to major incidents including floods, severe weather, wildfires, and hazardous materials releases. The emergency management plan follows the Incident Command System framework used across North America. The city maintains emergency operations centres at primary and backup locations staffed by representatives from police, fire, EMS, utilities, transit, and social services. Community emergency response plans are developed for each ward to address localized risks. Flood mitigation is a high priority following the 2013 flood, with investments in upstream storage on the Elbow River, permanent flood barriers along the Bow River, and property buyouts in the highest-risk floodway areas. The alert notification system sends warnings through mobile devices, social media, radio, and television. Annual exercises test coordination between agencies and identify gaps in response capabilities. The plan is reviewed every three years with input from community organizations and neighbouring municipalities.

title: Climate action plan
text: Calgary's climate action plan sets a net-zero greenhouse gas emissions target by 2050 with an interim target of 40 percent reduction by 2030 from 2005 levels. Buildings account for 58 percent of community emissions, followed by transportation at 33 percent and waste at 5 percent. The plan prioritizes building energy retrofits through a financing program that allows property owners to repay costs through their property tax bills. New buildings must meet higher energy performance standards starting in 2025. Transportation emissions reduction relies on increasing transit mode share, expanding cycling infrastructure, and supporting electric vehicle adoption through public charging stations. The city itself targets carbon neutral operations by 2040, including converting the municipal fleet to electric vehicles and powering city buildings with 100 percent renewable electricity. District energy systems in the downtown core and the East Village use waste heat from wastewater to heat buildings. A carbon budget tracks annual progress and triggers escalating policy actions if the city falls behind its trajectory.

title: Transportation infrastructure plan
text: Calgary's transportation infrastructure plan covers roads, bridges, interchanges, and active transportation facilities. The city manages over 16,000 lane-kilometres of roads and 450 bridges. Capital investment priorities include completing the ring road system with the remaining segments of Stoney Trail, grade-separating major rail crossings, and rebuilding deteriorating interchanges in the inner city. The complete streets policy requires all new road projects to accommodate pedestrians, cyclists, and transit alongside motor vehicles. Protected bike lanes have been built along 7th Street, 12th Avenue, and 5th Street in the downtown core, with network expansion planned to connect these routes to surrounding communities. Traffic signal optimization uses adaptive systems that adjust green times based on real-time detector data to reduce congestion. The snow and ice control policy clears priority routes within 24 hours of a snowfall event and residential streets within 7 days. Road maintenance uses a pavement condition index to prioritize rehabilitation spending where deterioration is most severe.

title: Business licensing and regulation
text: The City of Calgary requires business licences for most commercial activities operating within city limits. Licence categories include home-based businesses, retail stores, restaurants, personal services, and contractor trades. The application process involves a land use compatibility check, fire and health inspections where applicable, and payment of annual fees that range from 100 to 500 dollars depending on the business type. The business licence bylaw was updated in 2022 to simplify categories and reduce processing times from 15 days to 5 business days for low-risk applications. Food establishments must comply with Alberta Health Services food safety regulations and pass inspection before opening. Home occupation licences allow businesses to operate from residential properties subject to restrictions on signage, customer visits, and outdoor storage. The city provides a business support centre that offers one-on-one advisory sessions, workshops on regulatory compliance, and referrals to provincial and federal programs. Annual licence renewals are automated with email reminders sent 60 days before expiry.

title: Community safety strategy
text: Calgary's community safety strategy takes a prevention-first approach to reducing crime and improving public safety. The strategy coordinates efforts across the Calgary Police Service, community organizations, and social service agencies. Crime prevention through environmental design guidelines are applied to new developments to improve natural surveillance and territorial reinforcement. The city funds community safety investment programs that provide grants to neighbourhood organizations for initiatives such as youth mentoring, newcomer integration, and conflict resolution training. The community standards bylaw addresses nuisance issues including noise complaints, property maintenance, and encampments on public land. The Safe Communities Innovation Fund supports pilot projects testing new approaches to persistent safety challenges. The strategy sets targets for reducing property crime, violent crime, and calls for service related to social disorder. Annual community perception surveys track residents' feelings of safety in their neighbourhoods and in the downtown core.

title: Waste and recycling services
text: Calgary provides black cart garbage, blue cart recycling, and green cart composting collection to all residential properties. The blue cart program accepts paper, cardboard, plastics numbered 1 through 7, glass, and metal containers. The green cart program collects food scraps, yard waste, and soiled paper products for composting at the city's composting facility. The current waste diversion rate is 62 percent, with a target of 70 percent by 2025 through expanded recycling education and enforcement of contamination rules. The city operates three waste management facilities: Shepard Landfill, East Calgary Landfill, and the Spyhill recycling and composting depot. Hazardous household waste including paint, batteries, and electronics must be taken to designated drop-off locations and cannot be placed in curbside carts. The pay-as-you-throw pricing model charges higher fees for larger black carts to incentivize waste reduction. Community cleanup events are held twice annually in each ward to address illegal dumping and litter accumulation.

title: Economic development strategy
text: Calgary Economic Development leads the city's efforts to attract investment, support business growth, and diversify the local economy beyond oil and gas. The strategy identifies four priority sectors: technology and innovation, logistics and transportation, agribusiness and food, and creative industries. The city offers tax incentives for companies locating in the downtown core to address office vacancy rates that peaked at 32 percent during the energy downturn. The innovation ecosystem includes accelerators, co-working spaces, and partnerships with the University of Calgary and SAIT Polytechnic. A film and television production incentive has attracted major productions to the region. The small business support program provides micro-grants of up to 10,000 dollars for businesses in their first two years of operation. Trade missions and international partnerships connect Calgary companies with markets in Asia, Europe, and the United States. Annual economic indicators tracked include GDP growth, employment rate, business formation rate, and venture capital investment.

title: Snow and ice control policy
text: Calgary's snow and ice control policy outlines the city's approach to winter road maintenance across over 16,000 lane-kilometres of roads. Priority 1 routes include major arterials, transit routes, and emergency access roads, which are cleared within 24 hours of a snowfall event. Priority 2 routes are collector roads cleared within 48 hours. Residential streets are cleared within 7 days if accumulation exceeds 7 centimetres. The city operates a fleet of over 200 plows, graders, and sanders. Salt and gravel application follows environmental guidelines that limit chloride loading on waterways. Property owners are responsible for clearing sidewalks adjacent to their property within 24 hours after snowfall ends. The city enforces this requirement through a complaint-driven inspection program with fines starting at 150 dollars. The Snow Route parking ban activates during major events, requiring vehicles to be moved from designated routes within 72 hours to allow plowing. The annual snow and ice control budget is approximately 55 million dollars with a contingency reserve for severe winters.

title: Arts and culture policy
text: Calgary's arts and culture policy supports a vibrant creative sector through funding programs, public art investments, and cultural space development. The city allocates 1 percent of eligible capital project budgets to public art through the Public Art Policy established in 2004. Grants are available to individual artists, arts organizations, and cultural festivals through the Cultural Development Grant Program, which distributes approximately 8 million dollars annually. Major cultural facilities include Arts Commons performing arts centre, the Glenbow Museum, Studio Bell home of the National Music Centre, and the Contemporary Calgary art gallery. The cultural plan identifies the need for affordable studio and rehearsal space in the inner city as creative industries are displaced by rising rents. Cultural districts in Inglewood, Kensington, and 17th Avenue SW receive enhanced streetscape funding and signage. The city partners with Calgary Arts Development to coordinate investment in the arts ecosystem. Indigenous arts and cultural expression are supported through dedicated programs and consultation with Treaty 7 nations.

title: Property assessment and taxation
text: The City of Calgary assesses the value of all properties annually using market value as of July 1 of the prior year. Assessment methods include the direct comparison approach for residential properties, the income approach for commercial properties, and the cost approach for special-purpose properties. The total assessed value of Calgary properties exceeds 300 billion dollars. Property taxes are calculated by multiplying the assessed value by the municipal tax rate set annually by City Council through the budget process. The residential tax rate is approximately 3.7 mills while the non-residential rate is approximately 10.8 mills. Property owners can appeal their assessment through a complaint to the Assessment Review Board within 60 days of receiving their assessment notice. The city provides tax relief programs including the Senior Property Tax Deferral Program and the Tax Instalment Payment Plan that spreads payments over 12 months. Assessment accuracy is monitored through a ratio study comparing assessed values to actual sale prices, targeting a median ratio between 0.95 and 1.05.
